narrative:
As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical 3.21 average—and a respectable LSAT score of 35, compared to the usual 36.70. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, calculated a 53.1% probability that you would fail it. This tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to your application's rejection as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed right away.

The most influential factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is well below the average of 5.53; this suggests your later academic performance in school wasn't as competitive as it needs to be for strong bar exam odds. Countering that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which had a meaningful positive influence by exceeding the norm and signaling solid prior academic strength. Your LSAT score also nudged things toward success, though not as strongly, as it was slightly below average but still respectable.

In comparing your profile to typical students in the dataset, your lower Year 3 decile was the standout concern, far more decisive than your strengths in GPA and LSAT, which helped but couldn't fully offset it. Overall, the model saw your trajectory as riskier for the bar exam than most applicants who get approved.

To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year rankings into higher deciles, perhaps aiming for 5 or above to align with successful peers. You might also retake the LSAT to push closer to or above 37, amplifying that positive signal. Building a stronger narrative around your academic improvement through additional certifications or graduate prep courses could help reframe your profile for the next application cycle. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—keep pushing, as your foundation shows real potential.
